title: Folklore the Affliction: Fall of the Spire - Part One: Choosing Characters
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hy7L_a8J9z4

Daniel (@dungeondive) opens a week-long campaign series on **Folklore the Affliction**, focused on the newly-arrived *Fall of the Spire* expansion. The framing is unusual: not a Let's Play on camera (too many fiddly rules for live mistakes), but a *play it off-camera, return with adventure recaps* format — closer to battle reports than a play-through.

Most of the video is dedicated to walking through the six new expansion heroes: the **Viper Maiden** (Persian asp-cult assassin), the **Druidus** (rune-inscribing fairy-mist druid from another dimension), the **Gentleman** (1900s pugilist time-slipped through an antique mirror), the **Magus** (Zoroastrian stargazer who can overwrite world events), the **Huntress** (shaman who can spirit-walk into ghost form mid-combat), and the **Warlord** (whirling-blade dervish from the Sahara). Each has two pickable subclasses, starting abilities, bespoke shop items, and a lore tree. Daniel settles on a *femme fatale* party: Druidus + Huntress, picked partly on flavour and partly to cover healing.

The back half is house rules. Folklore is poorly balanced for two heroes — Daniel doubles coin rewards, splits the loot into four single-purpose decks, adds *target tokens* (a missed attack banks +5 for a later roll), grants the party 1d6 vita and 1d3 power after every skirmish, and most importantly bases enemy HP on the **five-player** stat divided by half, not the two-player stat (the game inexplicably gives enemies *more* HP with fewer heroes).

----

Daniel's most contentious house rule is recalculating enemy HP from the 5-player number rather than the 2-player number. When a game scales *up* in difficulty with fewer heroes, do you trust the designer's intent or assume it's a balance error?
